clinicalStem: A 72-year-old retired teacher is brought by her daughter who reports a 14-month history of progressive memory loss — initially forgetting appointments, now unable to recall conversations from the same day. She gets lost driving familiar routes and has difficulty managing her finances. She scores 21/30 on the MMSE (losing points on recall, orientation, and serial sevens). Her BP is 138/82 mmHg, BMI 24, and neurological examination reveals no focal deficits. She takes no regular medications. Which single investigation is the most appropriate FIRST step to identify a potentially reversible cause of her cognitive decline?
options: Serum TSH, FBC, renal function, LFTs, vitamin B12, folate, and glucose
Lumbar puncture for CSF amyloid-beta and tau proteins
explanation: The first step in any dementia workup is to exclude reversible/treatable metabolic and endocrine causes. A structured blood panel (TSH, FBC, B12, folate, renal, liver, glucose) identifies hypothyroidism, B12 deficiency, anaemia, and other correctable conditions. Advanced imaging and CSF biomarkers are appropriate later if reversible causes are excluded. MRI brain is also part of the standard workup but comes after blood tests.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRI brain is an important second-line investigation but should follow the blood screen; it will not identify metabolic reversible causes.
Correct — this is the standard first-line investigation panel to exclude reversible metabolic dementia causes.
CSF tau/amyloid biomarkers are specialised Alzheimer's confirmatory tests used after reversible causes are excluded; not the first step.
FDG-PET is a specialised imaging tool used in ambiguous cases or research settings, not a first-line investigation.
EEG has limited diagnostic yield in progressive dementia workup and is not a first-line test unless CJD or seizures are suspected.
clinicalPearl: Always exclude reversible causes of dementia (thyroid, B12, renal, hepatic, glucose) with a structured blood panel before pursuing advanced imaging or CSF biomarkers.
keyClue: Progressive 14-month cognitive decline with no focal neurology — the question asks for the FIRST step to identify a REVERSIBLE cause.
commonExamTrap: Jumping straight to MRI brain, which is important but comes after ruling out metabolic/reversible causes.
missedConcept: Reversible dementias must always be excluded first; up to 10–15% of apparent dementia cases have a treatable underlying cause.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old retired engineer presents with a 2-year history of insidious memory difficulties. He forgets names of close friends, misplaces objects, and recently could not remember a family holiday from the previous month. He is fully oriented to person but mildly disoriented to date. His wife reports mild word-finding difficulty in conversation. MMSE is 23/30. All blood investigations including TSH, B12, folate, FBC, renal and liver function are normal. MRI brain shows bilateral hippocampal and entorhinal cortex atrophy with no vascular lesions or mass. Which of the following CSF findings would best support a diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease?
options: Elevated CSF protein with normal glucose
Decreased amyloid-beta 42 with increased total tau and phospho-tau
Oligoclonal bands with elevated IgG index
explanation: The classic Alzheimer's CSF biomarker profile is decreased amyloid-beta 42 (due to deposition in plaques, reducing CSF levels) combined with increased total tau and phosphorylated tau (reflecting neuronal injury and neurofibrillary tangle formation). This pattern has high sensitivity and specificity for Alzheimer's pathology and is used in specialist centres to support diagnosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Elevated protein with normal glucose is a non-specific finding seen in Guillain-Barré, tumours, and other conditions — not characteristic of Alzheimer's.
Elevated 14-3-3 protein is the hallmark CSF finding in Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD), not Alzheimer's disease.
Correct — decreased Abeta42 and elevated tau/phospho-tau is the Alzheimer's CSF biomarker signature.
Pleocytosis with elevated pressure suggests meningitis or inflammatory/infectious CNS disease, not Alzheimer's.
Oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index are characteristic of multiple sclerosis, not Alzheimer's disease.
clinicalPearl: Alzheimer's CSF biomarkers: low amyloid-beta 42 (trapped in plaques) + high total tau + high phospho-tau — this triad strongly supports the diagnosis.
keyClue: Normal MRI findings other than hippocampal/entorhinal atrophy, normal metabolic workup — the question asks for the CSF profile supporting Alzheimer's.
commonExamTrap: Confusing elevated 14-3-3 protein (CJD) with Alzheimer's biomarkers.
missedConcept: Amyloid-beta 42 is low in CSF (not high) in Alzheimer's because it is sequestered in amyloid plaques in the brain parenchyma.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old university student is brought to the emergency department at 2 AM by his flatmates. Over the past 8 hours he developed severe headache, photophobia, neck stiffness, and fever of 39.4°C. On examination he is drowsy (GCS 13), has a positive Kernig's sign, and you notice a non-blanching petechial rash spreading over his trunk and lower limbs. His BP is 88/54 mmHg and HR is 118 bpm. What is the single most important immediate action?
options: Perform urgent CT head before any treatment
Perform lumbar puncture immediately to confirm diagnosis
Obtain blood cultures and wait for results before antibiotics
Arrange urgent MRI brain with gadolinium
explanation: This presentation is classic bacterial meningococcal meningitis with septicaemia (non-blanching petechial rash, haemodynamic instability). In suspected bacterial meningitis, antibiotics must NOT be delayed — IV ceftriaxone should be given immediately, before LP and before CT if there is any clinical concern. Every hour of delay in antibiotic treatment increases mortality significantly. The non-blanching rash indicating meningococcaemia makes this a life-threatening emergency.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CT head is needed before LP if there are signs of raised ICP, but antibiotics must be given first — CT must NEVER delay antibiotic administration in suspected meningitis.

LP is essential for diagnosis but must be deferred until after antibiotics are started and CT excludes raised ICP — LP before antibiotics in this unstable patient could cause herniation and delay life-saving treatment.
Correct — immediate IV ceftriaxone is the priority; antibiotic delay increases mortality and the non-blanching rash signals meningococcal septicaemia.

Waiting for blood culture results before giving antibiotics is dangerous and contraindicated in suspected bacterial meningitis — blood cultures take hours and mortality rises with every hour of delay.
MRI brain is not appropriate in an acute emergency and would cause dangerous delay; CT would be used to exclude raised ICP before LP, after antibiotics are started.
clinicalPearl: In suspected bacterial meningitis with haemodynamic instability or non-blanching rash, administer IV ceftriaxone IMMEDIATELY — never delay antibiotics for imaging or LP.
keyClue: Non-blanching petechial rash + fever + meningism + haemodynamic instability = meningococcal septicaemia requiring immediate antibiotics.
commonExamTrap: Performing CT or LP before giving antibiotics — investigations must NEVER delay antibiotics in suspected bacterial meningitis.
missedConcept: Non-blanching rash in the context of meningism signifies meningococcal septicaemia, a rapidly fatal condition where antibiotic delay is the leading preventable cause of death.
redFlagReason: Meningococcal meningitis with septicaemia (non-blanching rash, haemodynamic shock) is immediately life-threatening — antibiotic delay measurably increases mortality; this is a true medical emergency.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old right-handed woman presents with a 6-week history of progressive right-sided headaches that are worse on waking in the morning and slightly relieved by sitting up. She also reports two episodes of vomiting without nausea in the past week. On examination, fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilloedema. Neurological examination shows subtle left arm drift on pronator drift testing. She is alert and oriented. Which investigation should be performed next?
options: Lumbar puncture for opening pressure measurement
MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast
explanation: MRI brain with gadolinium is the investigation of choice for suspected intracranial tumour. It provides superior soft-tissue resolution compared to CT, can identify the lesion, its relationship to surrounding structures, degree of oedema, and enhancement pattern, and helps characterise the tumour type. Gadolinium enhancement distinguishes high-grade from low-grade tumours and identifies blood-brain barrier breakdown.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Lumbar puncture is CONTRAINDICATED here — bilateral papilloedema indicates raised intracranial pressure and LP could cause tonsillar herniation ('coning'). This is a dangerous option.
Correct — MRI brain with gadolinium is the gold standard investigation for suspected intracranial tumour, providing superior characterisation compared to CT.
Plain skull X-ray has very limited diagnostic value for intracranial tumours and would not identify a mass lesion; it is not used in modern practice for this indication.
CT without contrast may show a mass lesion but provides far less detail than MRI and misses many tumours, especially in the posterior fossa; gadolinium MRI is preferred.
Cerebral angiography is used for vascular abnormalities (aneurysms, AVM) and is not the appropriate first investigation for a suspected mass lesion.
clinicalPearl: Morning headache worse on lying down + papilloedema + focal deficit = raised ICP from a mass lesion; MRI with gadolinium is the gold standard investigation, and LP is contraindicated.
keyClue: Morning headaches worse on waking, projectile vomiting, bilateral papilloedema, and focal motor sign = raised ICP from intracranial mass.
commonExamTrap: Performing lumbar puncture in a patient with papilloedema — this risks fatal tonsillar herniation.
missedConcept: Bilateral papilloedema is a contraindication to LP until a mass lesion with raised ICP is excluded by neuroimaging.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman presents with a 5-day history of fever (38.8°C), headache, and increasingly bizarre behaviour — her husband reports she has been having vivid visual hallucinations and has become verbally aggressive, which is completely out of character. On day 4 she developed a generalised tonic-clonic seizure. Examination reveals mild neck stiffness and temporal lobe-predominant aphasia. MRI brain shows T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the right medial temporal lobe and insular cortex. CSF shows: WBC 85 (lymphocytes 90%), protein 0.9 g/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L (serum glucose 5.8). Which pathogen is most likely responsible?
explanation: HSV-1 encephalitis characteristically affects the medial temporal lobes and insular cortex, producing fever, personality/behavioural change, temporal lobe seizures, and aphasia. CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein and normal glucose — consistent with viral encephalitis. MRI showing unilateral or bilateral temporal/insular signal change is the hallmark of HSV encephalitis. PCR for HSV DNA in CSF is the diagnostic gold standard.
wrongAnswerExplanations: N. meningitidis causes bacterial meningitis (neutrophilic pleocytosis, low glucose, high protein) and does not typically cause the behavioural/temporal lobe syndrome described.
Correct — HSV-1 encephalitis classically presents with temporal lobe involvement, personality change, hallucinations, and seizures with this CSF and MRI pattern.
Listeria causes bacterial meningitis/rhombencephalitis, typically in the elderly or immunocompromised; it would show neutrophilic pleocytosis and low glucose.
Cryptococcal meningitis presents more subacutely with headache and raised ICP in immunocompromised patients; it does not produce the acute temporal behavioural syndrome.
TB meningitis has a subacute onset, low glucose (very low), high protein, and lymphocytic pleocytosis but lacks the acute temporal lobe syndrome and would not cause this MRI pattern.
clinicalPearl: HSV-1 encephalitis = fever + personality change + temporal lobe seizures + temporal/insular MRI signal change + lymphocytic CSF; treat empirically with IV aciclovir while awaiting PCR.
keyClue: MRI T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in medial temporal lobe and insula + behavioural change + seizures = HSV-1 encephalitis until proven otherwise.
commonExamTrap: Assuming bacterial meningitis because there is neck stiffness — the lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, and temporal MRI changes all point to viral encephalitis.
missedConcept: HSV encephalitis has a predilection for the limbic system (medial temporal lobes) and presents with psychiatric features and seizures, not just 'classic meningitis' symptoms.
redFlagReason: HSV encephalitis is rapidly fatal if untreated — IV aciclovir must be started empirically as soon as the diagnosis is suspected, without waiting for PCR results.

clinicalStem: A 78-year-old man with a 10-year history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes is brought by his son with 3 years of cognitive decline. His son reports that the decline has been 'stepwise' — there were periods of stability followed by sudden worsening after what seemed to be small strokes. He has noticeable slowing of gait, urinary urgency, and emotional lability. MMSE is 20/30 with particular difficulty on attention, processing speed tasks, and executive function. Memory recall is relatively less impaired than expected. Neurological examination reveals brisk reflexes and a mild left-sided weakness. MRI brain shows multiple small subcortical white matter hyperintensities and lacunar infarcts. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Vascular dementia is characterised by a stepwise deteriorating cognitive decline in the context of vascular risk factors (hypertension, diabetes), focal neurological signs, emotional lability, executive dysfunction (more than memory), and MRI showing white matter changes and lacunar infarcts. The stepwise (not insidious gradual) decline and imaging findings are the key distinguishing features from Alzheimer's disease.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Alzheimer's disease presents with insidious onset and gradual progressive decline (not stepwise), with early episodic memory impairment predominating — this patient's pattern and imaging do not fit.
Lewy body dementia presents with fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, Parkinsonism, and REM sleep behaviour disorder — none of these features are prominently described here.

Frontotemporal dementia typically presents in patients aged 45–65 with prominent personality change and executive dysfunction but relative memory sparing — it would not have this vascular history and MRI pattern.
Correct — stepwise decline + vascular risk factors + executive dysfunction + MRI white matter changes + focal neurology = vascular dementia.

Normal pressure hydrocephalus presents with the classic triad of gait apraxia, urinary incontinence, and cognitive decline (wet, wobbly, wacky) — while some features overlap, NPH would show ventricular enlargement disproportionate to cortical atrophy on MRI, not lacunar infarcts.
clinicalPearl: Stepwise cognitive decline + vascular risk factors + executive dysfunction > memory + MRI lacunar infarcts/white matter lesions = vascular dementia.
keyClue: Stepwise deterioration after apparent small strokes in a patient with hypertension and diabetes, with lacunar infarcts on MRI.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing Alzheimer's in an elderly patient with dementia — the stepwise pattern, vascular risk factors, and MRI findings clearly indicate vascular aetiology.
missedConcept: Vascular dementia affects executive function and processing speed early, while memory is relatively preserved compared to Alzheimer's, and the decline is stepwise rather than gradual.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man presents to the emergency department after a witnessed first generalised tonic-clonic seizure. His wife reports he has had progressive worsening right-sided headaches for 8 weeks and 2 weeks of subtle left-sided weakness. He has no past neurological history and takes antihypertensives only. Urgent CT head shows a heterogeneous right parietal mass with surrounding vasogenic oedema and midline shift of 7mm. Urgent MRI with gadolinium shows a ring-enhancing lesion with a necrotic centre, irregular margins, and finger-like projections of oedema into adjacent white matter ('butterfly' extension crossing the corpus callosum is absent). Spectroscopy shows markedly elevated choline-to-creatine ratio. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM, WHO grade IV glioma)
explanation: Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM) is the most common malignant primary brain tumour in adults, typically presenting in the 5th–6th decade with progressive headaches, focal deficits, and seizures. The MRI hallmark is an irregular ring-enhancing lesion with central necrosis, surrounding vasogenic oedema, and finger-like white matter infiltration. Elevated choline-to-creatine ratio on spectroscopy reflects high cellular turnover. Butterfly GBM crosses the corpus callosum but single-lobe lesions without that feature are more common.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Brain abscess is also ring-enhancing but tends to have smooth, thin regular walls, restricted diffusion (bright on DWI/dark on ADC) in the core, and is associated with fever, immunosuppression, or known source of infection — the spectroscopy pattern also differs.
Correct — irregular ring enhancement, necrotic core, finger-like oedema, elevated choline:creatine, age 58, progressive course = GBM until proven otherwise.
Primary CNS lymphoma is usually a homogeneously enhancing periventricular mass in immunocompromised patients; it does not typically have necrosis or irregular ring enhancement (unless treated).

Metastatic tumour is possible (ring-enhancing) but typically presents as multiple lesions at the grey-white matter junction; single lesion with finger-like infiltration and high choline favours primary glioma.

Subacute infarction can show ring enhancement (luxury perfusion) but would be in a vascular territory, have restricted diffusion acutely, and not show the elevated choline spectroscopy pattern of high-grade glioma.
clinicalPearl: GBM: irregular ring-enhancing mass with central necrosis, vasogenic oedema, finger-like white matter extension, and elevated choline:creatine on spectroscopy — most common malignant primary brain tumour in adults.
keyClue: Irregular ring-enhancing lesion with necrotic centre, finger-like white matter extension, and markedly elevated choline:creatine spectroscopy in a 58-year-old.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing brain abscess (also ring-enhancing) — abscess has smooth thin walls and DWI restriction, whereas GBM has irregular walls, no DWI restriction in core, and high choline.
missedConcept: Glioblastoma is a primary neuroectodermal tumour that infiltrates along white matter tracts, producing finger-like oedema patterns; central necrosis results from outgrowing its blood supply.

clinicalStem: A 65-year-old woman is referred by her GP with an 18-month history of gradual memory decline. She forgets recent conversations and has difficulty learning new information, though remote memory is relatively preserved. She scores 24/30 on the MMSE (mainly losing recall points). All routine bloods including TSH, B12, FBC, and metabolic panel are normal. CT brain shows generalised cerebral atrophy. Her neurologist suspects early Alzheimer's disease and wants to proceed with functional neuroimaging to detect the characteristic pattern. Which investigation would best demonstrate the typical hypometabolism pattern in Alzheimer's disease?
explanation: FDG-PET (fluorodeoxyglucose PET) measures cerebral glucose metabolism and is the gold-standard functional imaging for detecting the pattern of hypometabolism in Alzheimer's disease. The characteristic pattern shows reduced FDG uptake in the temporoparietal cortices, posterior cingulate cortex, and precuneus — regions involved early in Alzheimer's pathology. This pattern helps distinguish AD from other dementias and can detect changes earlier than structural MRI.
wrongAnswerExplanations: SPECT with Tc-99m HMPAO measures cerebral blood flow and can show temporoparietal hypoperfusion in AD, but FDG-PET is more sensitive and specific and is considered the gold standard for metabolic imaging.
Correct — FDG-PET shows characteristic temporoparietal and posterior cingulate hypometabolism in Alzheimer's disease and is the gold standard functional imaging investigation.

Amyloid PET detects amyloid plaque burden and can support the diagnosis, but it measures amyloid deposition rather than hypometabolism — it is a separate biomarker and is used as a confirmatory test, not to demonstrate the metabolic pattern.
CT brain with contrast shows structural changes only and cannot demonstrate metabolic activity or functional patterns characteristic of Alzheimer's.
fMRI measures BOLD signal changes during tasks rather than baseline metabolism and is primarily a research tool, not used clinically to diagnose Alzheimer's disease.
clinicalPearl: FDG-PET in Alzheimer's disease shows temporoparietal and posterior cingulate hypometabolism — it detects functional changes earlier than structural MRI and can help distinguish AD from other dementias.
keyClue: The question asks for the investigation that best demonstrates hypometabolism — FDG-PET is the metabolic imaging gold standard.
commonExamTrap: Confusing amyloid PET (detects plaques) with FDG-PET (detects hypometabolism) — they are different biomarkers answering different questions.
missedConcept: In Alzheimer's, hypometabolism on FDG-PET in the temporoparietal and posterior cingulate regions can precede structural atrophy on MRI by years.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old man known to be HIV-positive (last CD4 count 45 cells/microL, not on antiretroviral therapy) presents with a 3-week history of progressively worsening headache, low-grade fever (37.9°C), photophobia, and increasing confusion. He has no neck stiffness or focal neurological signs. CT head is normal. Lumbar puncture results: opening pressure 32 cmH2O (elevated), WBC 8/mm3 (predominantly lymphocytes), protein 0.65 g/L, glucose 2.1 mmol/L (serum 5.2). What is the single best confirmatory test from the CSF for the most likely diagnosis?
options: CSF Gram stain and bacterial culture
CSF India ink preparation and cryptococcal antigen (CrAg)
explanation: Cryptococcal meningitis is the most likely diagnosis in an HIV-positive patient with CD4 <100 cells/microL presenting with subacute meningitis, raised intracranial pressure, and minimal CSF pleocytosis. India ink staining demonstrates the encapsulated yeast (negative staining of the capsule), and CSF cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) is >99% sensitive and specific for cryptococcal meningitis, making this the confirmatory investigation of choice.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Gram stain and bacterial culture are appropriate for suspected bacterial meningitis; the subacute course, HIV status with low CD4, and CSF profile (minimal pleocytosis, elevated pressure) make bacterial meningitis much less likely here.
CSF HSV PCR would be indicated for suspected HSV encephalitis (typically presenting with behavioural change, seizures, temporal lobe signs) — not the subacute meningitis syndrome described.
Correct — India ink + CSF CrAg is the confirmatory investigation for cryptococcal meningitis in an HIV patient with CD4 <100 cells/microL and subacute meningitis.

CSF VDRL tests for neurosyphilis, which can cause a subacute meningitis but is less likely than cryptococcal meningitis in this setting; it should be part of the workup but is not the best single confirmatory test here.
CSF cytology is used to diagnose leptomeningeal malignancy (carcinomatous meningitis); though possible, the clinical picture here strongly favours cryptococcal infection over malignancy.
clinicalPearl: In HIV with CD4 <100 and subacute meningitis with high opening pressure and minimal pleocytosis, always consider cryptococcal meningitis — confirm with India ink and CSF CrAg (>99% sensitive).
keyClue: HIV-positive, CD4 45 cells/microL, subacute meningitis, elevated ICP, minimal CSF pleocytosis = cryptococcal meningitis until proven otherwise.
commonExamTrap: Missing cryptococcal meningitis because neck stiffness is absent — in immunocompromised patients, inflammatory signs may be minimal even with serious CNS infection.
missedConcept: Cryptococcal meningitis in advanced HIV presents with minimal CSF pleocytosis because the immune response is suppressed; elevated opening pressure and high CrAg titre are the key diagnostic features.
redFlagReason: Untreated cryptococcal meningitis is rapidly fatal in immunocompromised patients; elevated ICP (32 cmH2O) can cause herniation — prompt diagnosis and treatment with amphotericin B + flucytosine is life-saving.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman is referred after her GP ordered a CT head for progressively worsening headaches over 6 months. She also reports two episodes of focal right arm jerking lasting 1–2 minutes (no loss of consciousness). The CT shows a well-defined, homogeneous, extra-axial mass arising from the left frontal convexity with a broad base against the dura, causing compression of the adjacent frontal lobe. There is a 'sunburst' calcification pattern within the lesion. Contrast CT shows intense homogeneous enhancement. The underlying brain appears displaced but not invaded. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Meningioma arises from arachnoid cap cells of the meninges and is characterised on imaging by: extra-axial location (outside the brain parenchyma), broad dural base, intense homogeneous contrast enhancement, calcification (sunburst or psammomatous), and a 'dural tail' sign. The brain is displaced rather than invaded. It is the most common benign intracranial tumour, more frequent in middle-aged women.
wrongAnswerExplanations: GBM is an intra-axial tumour arising within brain parenchyma; it is heterogeneous with ring enhancement and necrosis, not homogeneous with a dural base — the imaging characteristics do not fit.
Brain abscess is intra-axial, ring-enhancing with thin smooth walls and DWI restriction; it would not have calcification or a broad dural base.
Correct — extra-axial, broad dural base, sunburst calcification, intense homogeneous enhancement = classic meningioma.
Primary CNS lymphoma is typically periventricular, homogeneously enhancing (in immunocompetent patients), and intra-axial — it does not arise from the dura.

Dural metastasis is possible but would typically have a known primary cancer history, multiple lesions, and irregular enhancement; the sunburst calcification and uniform enhancement point to meningioma.
clinicalPearl: Meningioma hallmarks: extra-axial, broad dural base, homogeneous enhancement, calcification, displaced (not invaded) brain, and 'dural tail' sign — most common benign intracranial tumour.
keyClue: Extra-axial mass with broad dural base, sunburst calcification, and intense homogeneous enhancement = meningioma.
commonExamTrap: Assuming any enhancing brain mass is GBM — meningioma is extra-axial (outside brain) whereas GBM is intra-axial; the location and homogeneity distinguish them.
missedConcept: Meningiomas are extra-axial (arising from the meninges) — the underlying brain is compressed but not infiltrated; this distinguishes them from primary glial tumours.

clinicalStem: A 74-year-old retired man is referred by his GP for a 12-month history of gradually worsening gait difficulty described as shuffling with a broad base and magnetic quality (feet difficult to lift off the floor). His wife notes mild cognitive slowing and forgetfulness over the same period. He also has urinary urgency and two episodes of urinary incontinence in the past month. There is no history of head injury, meningitis, or subarachnoid haemorrhage. His blood pressure is 130/78 mmHg. CT brain shows enlarged lateral and third ventricles out of proportion to the degree of cortical sulcal widening. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH) presents with the classic triad of gait apraxia (magnetic, broad-based shuffling gait), cognitive impairment, and urinary incontinence — the 'wet, wobbly, and wacky' triad. CT/MRI characteristically shows ventriculomegaly out of proportion to cortical atrophy (Evans index >0.3), distinguishing it from cerebral atrophy. NPH is important to diagnose because it is a potentially reversible dementia treated with ventriculoperitoneal shunting.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Alzheimer's disease causes gradual memory-prominent cognitive decline without the characteristic gait apraxia or urinary symptoms early in the course; MRI shows hippocampal/cortical atrophy, not disproportionate ventriculomegaly.

Vascular dementia presents with stepwise decline in the context of vascular risk factors and MRI showing lacunar infarcts/white matter disease — the imaging and the absence of vascular history do not support this.
Correct — the triad of magnetic gait apraxia + cognitive decline + urinary incontinence with disproportionate ventriculomegaly = NPH, a potentially reversible dementia.
Parkinson's disease causes a resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and a festinating (not magnetic) gait; urinary incontinence is a late feature and imaging does not show ventriculomegaly.

Subdural haematoma can cause cognitive decline and gait disturbance but typically follows head injury, may show fluctuating consciousness, and CT would show a crescent-shaped hypodense collection, not ventriculomegaly.
clinicalPearl: NPH triad: 'Wet, Wobbly, Wacky' — urinary incontinence, magnetic gait apraxia, and cognitive impairment with disproportionate ventriculomegaly on imaging; it is a treatable/reversible cause of dementia.
keyClue: Magnetic broad-based gait + urinary incontinence + cognitive decline + CT ventriculomegaly disproportionate to cortical atrophy = NPH.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing Parkinson's disease because of gait shuffling — NPH gait is broad-based and 'magnetic' (feet stuck to floor), while Parkinson's gait is narrow-based and festinating.
missedConcept: NPH is a potentially reversible dementia — early recognition matters because ventriculoperitoneal shunting can improve or reverse the triad, especially gait.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman originally from a TB-endemic region presents with a 4-week history of low-grade fever, night sweats, progressive headache, and weight loss. Over the past week she has developed confusion and diplopia. She is mildly immunocompromised following renal transplant 2 years ago. On examination she has bilateral papilloedema and right 6th cranial nerve palsy. CT head shows basal meningeal enhancement. Lumbar puncture results: opening pressure 28 cmH2O, WBC 210/mm3 (lymphocytes 88%), protein 2.8 g/L, glucose 1.4 mmol/L (serum glucose 5.6 mmol/L). Which investigation performed on the CSF has the highest sensitivity for confirming the underlying diagnosis?
options: CSF Ziehl-Neelsen (ZN) stain for acid-fast bacilli
explanation: CSF Mycobacterium tuberculosis PCR (GeneXpert MTB/RIF) has the highest sensitivity among CSF tests for TB meningitis, significantly outperforming the Ziehl-Neelsen smear (which is only 10–40% sensitive in CSF). GeneXpert MTB/RIF on CSF has a sensitivity of approximately 60–80% and provides results within 2 hours while also detecting rifampicin resistance. TB culture is the gold standard for confirmation but takes 4–8 weeks, making PCR the best rapid confirmatory test.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CSF Gram stain is used for bacterial meningitis and would be negative for TB meningitis; it has no role in confirming tuberculous infection.
ZN stain for acid-fast bacilli in CSF has very low sensitivity (10–40%) because the bacterial load is often low in TBM — while it is performed, it is not the most sensitive test.
Correct — CSF GeneXpert MTB/RIF PCR is the most sensitive rapid test for TB meningitis, detecting mycobacterial DNA and rifampicin resistance within 2 hours.
India ink staining is used to diagnose cryptococcal meningitis, not TB meningitis; it would not be the appropriate confirmatory test here.

CSF VDRL detects syphilitic meningitis (neurosyphilis); while syphilis can cause a similar subacute meningitis syndrome, the TB-endemic origin, constitutional features, and basal enhancement make TB far more likely.
clinicalPearl: For TB meningitis, CSF GeneXpert MTB/RIF PCR provides the best sensitivity (60–80%) with rapid results and rifampicin resistance detection — it outperforms ZN smear (10–40% sensitivity) for CSF.
keyClue: 4-week subacute febrile illness + immunocompromised + TB-endemic origin + basal meningeal enhancement + low CSF glucose + lymphocytic pleocytosis = TB meningitis; GeneXpert is the most sensitive rapid test.
commonExamTrap: Choosing ZN stain as the most sensitive test — it is traditionally taught but has poor sensitivity for CSF samples; PCR has superseded it in clinical practice.
missedConcept: In TB meningitis, CSF glucose is characteristically very low (<2.2 mmol/L or CSF:serum ratio <0.5), protein is markedly elevated, and the clinical course is subacute over weeks, unlike acute bacterial meningitis.
